raw_response:
{
    "internal_reasoning": "This appraisal concerns *Memories of Passersby I* (2018) by Mario Klingemann, a seminal work in the history of Artificial Intelligence art. Klingemann is widely recognized as a pioneer in the field, often referred to as a 'godfather' of AI art, making his early physical installations highly significant to collectors of media art and contemporary technology-based works. \n\nArtistically, the piece is an autonomous machine that utilizes a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to create an endless stream of unique, non-existent portraits. Unlike a video loop, the work is 'alive,' generating novel imagery in real-time. The aesthetic deliberately evokes the ghostly, textured quality of 17th and 18th-century oil portraiture, creating a dialogue between classical art history and futuristic algorithmic generation. The faces often drift into the 'uncanny valley,' possessing a haunting beauty that challenges our perception of humanity and memory. \n\nFrom a market perspective, this specific work has a notable auction history. It was featured in Sotheby's London sale 'Curating the AI Art' in March 2019, where it realized £40,625 (approximately $53,000 USD at the time). This sale was pivotal, occurring just months after the record-breaking sale of *Portrait of Edmond de Belamy* at Christie's, cementing AI art as a viable asset class. Since 2019, the market for AI and digital art has matured significantly. While the initial hype has settled, the value of 'blue-chip' historical works by pioneers like Klingemann has stabilized and appreciated among institutional and high-net-worth collectors who view these pieces as the 'early video art' of the 21st century (akin to Nam June Paik). \n\nThe physical presentation—dual high-resolution screens mounted above a mid-century modern wooden cabinet housing the computational 'brain'—adds a sculptural, furniture-like quality that appeals to interior collectors, distinct from purely screen-based digital art. \n\nCondition is a critical factor for media art. The valuation assumes the hardware (computer, screens) is functional and that the software/code has been preserved or migrated to current operating systems, a common issue with tech-based art (obsolescence). Assuming the work is in good working order and includes the necessary documentation for future maintenance, its value has likely appreciated due to Klingemann's sustained reputation and the exploding cultural relevance of AI. I estimate the current fair market value to be nearly double the 2019 realization, reflecting its status as a museum-quality historical artifact of the AI revolution.",
    "valuation_usd": 95000,
    "confidence": 0.85,
    "key_factors": [
        "Artist Reputation (Mario Klingemann as AI Pioneer)",
        "Historical Significance (Early Autonomous GAN Installation)",
        "Auction Provenance (Sotheby's 2019 Sale)",
        "Physical/ sculptural nature of the installation",
        "Current Market Demand for AI Art"
    ]
}

parsed.internal_reasoning:
This appraisal concerns *Memories of Passersby I* (2018) by Mario Klingemann, a seminal work in the history of Artificial Intelligence art. Klingemann is widely recognized as a pioneer in the field, often referred to as a 'godfather' of AI art, making his early physical installations highly significant to collectors of media art and contemporary technology-based works. 

Artistically, the piece is an autonomous machine that utilizes a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to create an endless stream of unique, non-existent portraits. Unlike a video loop, the work is 'alive,' generating novel imagery in real-time. The aesthetic deliberately evokes the ghostly, textured quality of 17th and 18th-century oil portraiture, creating a dialogue between classical art history and futuristic algorithmic generation. The faces often drift into the 'uncanny valley,' possessing a haunting beauty that challenges our perception of humanity and memory. 

From a market perspective, this specific work has a notable auction history. It was featured in Sotheby's London sale 'Curating the AI Art' in March 2019, where it realized £40,625 (approximately $53,000 USD at the time). This sale was pivotal, occurring just months after the record-breaking sale of *Portrait of Edmond de Belamy* at Christie's, cementing AI art as a viable asset class. Since 2019, the market for AI and digital art has matured significantly. While the initial hype has settled, the value of 'blue-chip' historical works by pioneers like Klingemann has stabilized and appreciated among institutional and high-net-worth collectors who view these pieces as the 'early video art' of the 21st century (akin to Nam June Paik). 

The physical presentation—dual high-resolution screens mounted above a mid-century modern wooden cabinet housing the computational 'brain'—adds a sculptural, furniture-like quality that appeals to interior collectors, distinct from purely screen-based digital art. 

Condition is a critical factor for media art. The valuation assumes the hardware (computer, screens) is functional and that the software/code has been preserved or migrated to current operating systems, a common issue with tech-based art (obsolescence). Assuming the work is in good working order and includes the necessary documentation for future maintenance, its value has likely appreciated due to Klingemann's sustained reputation and the exploding cultural relevance of AI. I estimate the current fair market value to be nearly double the 2019 realization, reflecting its status as a museum-quality historical artifact of the AI revolution.
key_factors: Artist Reputation (Mario Klingemann as AI Pioneer)
Physical/ sculptural nature of the installation
Current Market Demand for AI Art
